questions.question: In an immersion setup, the transducer is built without a wear plate or wedge because:
options: The element is too fragile to protect
The water path itself serves as the coupling medium
Immersion probes never use piezoelectric elements
A wedge would prevent any beam angle
questions.explanation: An immersion probe couples through the water column, so it needs a waterproof, acoustically matched face rather than a contact wear plate or wedge.

questions.question: Tank water for immersion testing is filtered, treated, and often de-aerated mainly to:
options: Raise the velocity of sound in the water
Prevent particles, biological growth, and air bubbles from scattering or blocking the beam
Increase the test frequency
Eliminate the front-surface echo
questions.explanation: Particles and bubbles scatter or block sound and create false signals, and untreated water grows algae and corrodes equipment, so the water is conditioned for clean coupling.

questions.question: A wheel (tire) transducer is advantageous because:
options: It can only perform through-transmission
The internal element can be aimed for straight-beam or angle-beam work while the wheel rolls along the part
It needs no liquid inside the tire
It requires the part to be fully submerged
questions.explanation: The element is mounted inside a liquid-filled tire and can be set for straight or angled beams, combining consistent immersion coupling with rolling mobility on production lines.

questions.question: For a 19 mm (¾ in.) steel plate, the minimum immersion water path (steel ≈ 5,920 m/s, water ≈ 1,480 m/s) is approximately:
options: 5 mm
19 mm
38 mm
76 mm
questions.explanation: Minimum water path = thickness × (v_water ÷ v_part) = 19 ÷ 4 ≈ 5 mm, with about 6 mm (¼ in.) of margin added in practice. (76 mm — multiplying by 4 — is the inverted rule.)

questions.question: On an immersion A-scan of a steel plate, the large echo seen before any interior signals is the:
options: Back-wall echo
Front-surface (entry) echo from the water-to-metal interface
A flaw echo from a lamination
Transmitted pulse from a second transducer
questions.explanation: Sound crosses the water path and reflects strongly off the front face of the part, producing the front-surface echo ahead of the back wall and any internal flaw echoes.

questions.question: Why does a focused immersion probe's focal point fall at a shallower depth in steel than its rating in water?
options: Steel attenuates the beam so it cannot reach the rated depth
The higher velocity of steel refracts the rays, shortening the focus by roughly the velocity ratio
The probe frequency drops in steel
The water path is added to the focal length
questions.explanation: The rated focal length is in water; entering the higher-velocity steel refracts the converging rays and shortens the focal distance by approximately the velocity ratio.

questions.question: To inspect the convex outer surface of a pipe with a normal-incidence immersion beam and overcome the surface's diverging effect, the best approach is to:
options: Use a flat probe and a very short water path
Aim the beam through the pipe's center and use a probe focused to match the radius
Increase the pulse repetition frequency
Tilt the probe well off the radial line
questions.explanation: A convex surface diverges the beam; aiming radially through the center and selecting a focus matched to the curvature restores a concentrated, useful beam.

questions.question: Lamb (plate) waves are used primarily to inspect:
options: Thick forgings and billets
Thin sheet and bonded panels, where the wave travels along the material
The bore of heavy-wall pipe
Cast iron engine blocks
questions.explanation: Plate waves form where thickness is comparable to a wavelength and travel along thin sheet, making them well suited to thin skins and bonded panels for disbonds and cracks.

questions.question: An immersion through-transmission setup shows a sudden drop in the received signal as the part moves under the nozzles. This most likely indicates:
options: The water path is too long
A flaw in the beam path is blocking or scattering the sound, or coupling has been lost
The reflector is exactly 25 mm deep
The frequency has doubled
questions.explanation: Through-transmission reads transmitted-beam strength; a flaw in the path or a coupling loss reduces the received signal, though it cannot indicate the flaw's depth.

questions.question: Which job is the strongest candidate for immersion rather than contact testing?
options: A single corroded elbow inspected in place on a live process line
A high-volume rolled-plate line requiring repeatable coverage and a permanent C-scan record
A field butt weld reachable only from one side on scaffolding
A quick thickness check on a tank wall during a walkdown
questions.explanation: High volume, repeatable automated coverage, and a recorded image are immersion's strengths; the other tasks are portable, one-sided, in-place work suited to contact testing.
